stops.title: [Waymo](https://waymo.com/)
stops.summary: Mobility platform using crowdsourced data, mapping, and logistics algorithms to move people
stops.takeaways: - Find the one thing that you're really into and go crazy, go deep - Bring your interests together, be a translator at your nexus “Being a translator at the point of your nexus”

stops.title: [Gensler](https://www.gensler.com/offices/san-francisco)
stops.summary: Global architecture, design, and planning firm
stops.takeaways: - Intentional design shapes behavior and outcomes - Physical space is a strategic tool - Strong team-based workflow - Integrates sustainability but through frameworks like LEED - Heavy internal research + client data “Intentional design generates results”, “No one does it alone”

stops.title: [Gehl](https://www.gehlpeople.com/)
stops.summary: Urban design + research firm focused on human-centered cities, public life studies, and behavior-driven planning
stops.takeaways: - While the built environment operates in silos, it is not experienced as silos - Cities must be designed at the human scale - Ethnographic research is powerful but hard to sell - Storytelling + data visualization drive decisions - Focus on lived experience, not just metrics; the data does not need to be novelty - Iteration + user feedback is critical - Everyone has data, what matters is the narrative you use to tell the story “Human beings are the most sophisticated sensor”, “The most sustainable building is one that is already built”

stops.title: [IDEO](https://www.ideo.com/)
stops.summary: Global design consultancy focusing on product, service, and experience design using human-centered design methods
stops.takeaways: - Strong client-driven work limits agency - Must clearly define “who you are designing for” - Balancing accessibility vs targeted design is key - Emphasis on desirability, feasibility, viability (+ responsibility) “Who are the humans we are designing for?”, "Have an open mind for opportunities to learn, you’ll learn when you need to but putting yourself in that position is up to you", "If you want to chance the industry, you have to know it"

stops.title: [City of San Francisco](https://www.sf.gov/departments--city-administrator--digital-services)
stops.summary: Civic tech and government focused on improving public services
stops.takeaways: - Civic tech constrained by hiring + procurement - Deep user research (even informal) is critical - Trust-building enables insight - Get to know the people that are experiencing the problems you are trying to solve Go get tacos

stops.title: [Good Neighbor Lab](https://www.goodneighborlab.org/)
stops.summary: Community-focused initiative using tech + engagement to strengthen neighborhoods
stops.takeaways: - Lead with joy and human connection - Community tech works better outside government in low-trust contexts - Breadth vs depth is a strategic choice - Bring in partners to add depth - Feedback should shape community innovation Interdisciplinary work builds on itself, diving into your interests will help your other interests get stronger

stops.title: [SF Bay Ferry](https://sanfranciscobayferry.com/)
stops.summary: Regional ferry transit system investing in electrification and sustainable mobility
stops.takeaways: - Infrastructure projects require massive coordination - Sustainability requires systems-level thinking - Service design must match evolving users - Sustainable development in infrastructure is rarely about an individual system doing everything right “It’s all interconnected”

stops.title: [SPUR](https://www.spur.org/)
stops.summary: Policy think tank focused on urban planning, governance, and civic systems in the Bay Area
stops.takeaways: - Civic tech blocked by procurement systems - Engagement is often the real problem, not apathy - Change must be local and trust-based “'People don’t care' is false — you’re just not engaging them correctly”

stops.title: [CA Forever](https://californiaforever.com/)
stops.summary: Private initiative to build a new city in California from scratch
stops.takeaways: - Biggest barrier is governance and permissions - Designing from scratch introduces new risks - Metrics like walkability + affordability guide vision The biggest challenge to building a city is permission

stops.title: [SITELAB](https://www.sitelaburbanstudio.com/)
stops.summary: Urban design studio focusing on public-interest projects and process-driven design
stops.takeaways: - Process and narrative are as important as outcome, the work sells itself - Risk mitigation (climate, legal) must be embedded early - Values: Curious, humble, ambitious “Don’t wait for your business cards to start your business - Instead of thinking about the what first, the why and how dictates who should be included in the conversation and what the “what” is”
